items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I’m again tormented
by vague suspicions…
Shpak has a tape recorder,
the envoy has a medallion…
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: The translation largely preserves the mock-heroic phrasing (“tormented by vague suspicions”), which is the core comedic register. However, it misses the key nuance noted in the reference: «У Шпака — …, у посла — …» here points to each having LOST an item (a parallel between thefts), not simply possessing it. Rendering it as “has” weakens the logic of the suspicion and blunts the callback joke (it sounds like he’s just listing who owns what, not who was robbed of what). Also, “envoy” is acceptable, but the reference’s “ambassador” better matches the comedic ‘official’ framing; still, that’s secondary.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
So nice, Tsar!
So nice—hello, Tsar!

Tsar, so nice!
Hello, Tsar! So nice!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The translation keeps the repetitive, awkward politeness that creates the era-clash humor, and it remains intelligible and comic. However, it slightly underplays the specific gag of Bunsha repeatedly “introducing himself” as the Tsar in a banal Soviet-social way (the reference’s “I’m the Tsar, very pleased…”). “So nice” reads more like casual friendliness than stiff formulaic politeness; “very pleased” / “pleased to meet you” would better evoke the Soviet cliché and the absurdity of repeating “tsar” as if it were a job title in a meet-and-greet.

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical in effect and essentially identical in wording; preserves the sudden authoritarian, power-hungry royal persona. No issues.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I’m in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Matches the reference closely and preserves the archaic/high-register melancholy that makes the line funny in a modern apartment context. No issues.

items.translation:
They walled me in!
They walled me in, demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Captures the meaning and the comic paranoia. Minor punctuation/ellipsis difference doesn’t matter; “demons” as addressee/agent is preserved. No issues.

items.translation: So that’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Meaning is correct and the religious framing is preserved, but the stylistic punch is softened. The reference’s “Behold the power…” carries a more elevated, proclamatory, quasi-biblical tone that heightens the joke (religious certainty applied to elevator mechanics). “So that’s what … can do” is more conversational and less ‘tsar-like’/grandiloquent.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: The line is understandable and functions as a command to start dancing, but it loses the specific ‘announcer/MC’ flavor of the Russian (a set phrase meaning “Everyone’s dancing!” / “All dance!”) and the comedic sense of a formal proclamation opening the dance floor. “Everybody dance!” sounds like a plain imperative rather than a catchphrase-like announcement; the era-clash and performative tone are reduced.

items.translation: What a lucky time to drop by!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Conveys the core meaning (self-satisfied delight at a fortuitous visit) and keeps the breezy criminal charm. Slightly less idiomatic/snappy than the reference, but still works comedically and naturally in English.

items.original: Икра черная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar…
And! Exotic caviar…

Eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: This misses the key creative constraint: the reference explicitly warns not to call the eggplant spread “caviar” in English because it confuses viewers and blunts the Soviet-satire inversion. The actual translation does exactly that (“Exotic caviar… Eggplant!”), which risks the audience thinking it’s literally eggplant caviar rather than a cheap spread being absurdly elevated. Also, “zamorskaya” is better as “overseas” (a set comic epithet), not “exotic,” which shifts the flavor from mock-official/advertising cliché to generic description.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Everything, everything I earned
by backbreaking labor—

all gone: 3 tape recorders,
3 film cameras,

3 domestic cigarette cases,
three suede jackets.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: The list structure and escalating-count humor are damaged. In the source, Shpak says “куртка… замшевая… три” (one jacket, then he corrects/ups the count to three), which is part of the comic inflation and his muddled counting. The translation collapses this into “three suede jackets” without preserving the hesitation and the reveal that he’s inflating/losing track. Also, “domestic” is attached to the cigarette cases in the source; that part is fine, but “movie cameras” vs “film cameras” is not important. The main loss is the comedic timing and the incremental self-exposure in the counting.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!

And they’ll cure you too!

And they’ll cure me!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Matches the reference essentially verbatim in meaning and rhythm; preserves the calm, sing-song resignation that makes the joke land. No issues.

summary.interpretation: 3.55 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.71 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
